title: Fitting a threshold strip
subtitle: Cover the floor-to-floor joint at a doorway
excerpt: How to select, cut, and fix a threshold strip at the junction between two floor types in a doorway: measuring the gap, choosing the correct profile, cutting to width, and fixing with screws or adhesive.
sourceNotes: Standard UK flooring practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: protects the exposed edge of a floor at the doorway where it meets a different floor in the next room. Without it, carpet frays, laminate lifts, and the visual join is untidy. Threshold profiles come in aluminium, brass, and timber, and in several cross-section shapes for different floor-height combinations.
Both floors at the same level. A flat aluminium or timber strip screwed to the subfloor covers the gap.
A T-profile sits in a channel. Neither floor is fixed; both can move. Used between two laminate floors or a laminate and an engineered-wood floor.
One floor higher than the other. A sloped profile transitions between them. Common between a thick tile floor and a thinner laminate.
A gripping bar that hooks down over the carpet edge and is screwed to the floor on the hard-floor side.

Measure the clear width between the door stops at floor level. Most threshold strips are sold at 900 mm and need cutting down. Mark the cut line and cut with the tenon saw, holding the strip in a vice or against a bench stop.

Lay the strip across the doorway with the centre over the joint between the two floors. The strip should cover the gap fully and extend slightly onto each floor surface. Mark the screw-hole positions on the floor with a pencil.

On a timber subfloor, drill pilot holes and drive the screws through the threshold strip directly. On a concrete screed, drill with the cordless drill and a 6 mm masonry bit, insert plastic wall plugs, and drive the screws in. Do not overtighten; threshold strips are hollow-backed and will crack if the screw is driven hard.
Fitting a T-bar on a floating floor

T-bars come with a separate fixing track. The track is screwed or glued to the subfloor in the doorway; the T-bar clips or slides into the track. Leave a 5 mm gap between the floor ends and the centre of the track on each side to allow for thermal movement. Do not fix the T-bar itself to the floor; it must be free to move with the floating floor.
Solid hardwood threshold pieces are mitred at 45 degrees at the doorway corners for a clean finish in period-style floors. They are cut, fitted, glued, and pinned in the same sequence as architrave.

Where drilling through an existing floor would break a moisture barrier (for example, on a concrete floor with a damp-proof membrane above), use a construction adhesive applied to the underside of the threshold strip rather than screwing through the floor.

attrs.fix: Pack below the strip with a thin strip of floor adhesive or flexible sealant before re-fixing. Ensure all screws reach solid material below.
attrs.cause: The floor beneath is uneven or one screw is not reaching solid fixing.
attrs.symptom: Threshold strip rocks when stood on

attrs.fix: Press the T-bar firmly back into the track along its full length. If the track screws have loosened, re-tighten or re-plug and re-fix before refitting the T-bar.
attrs.cause: The T-bar clip has come loose from the fixing track.
